data.title: 6 Upcoming Dividend Increases Including A King
data.description:
Welcome back to another edition of upcoming dividend increases. I'll focus on dividend increases for the week of September 28th, 2025. I encourage investors to consider the power of dividend stocks, your pathway to steady and increasing income. There's nothing like sitting back and collecting regular paychecks from everyday companies simply by holding their shares. Adopting a dividend growth strategy encourages your returns to grow, making your financial future brighter. Now is a great time to invest in dividend stocks and enjoy the financial rewards they bring.

Companies that regularly raise their dividends tend to be great long-term investments. Dividend increases suggest financial strength and stability, indicating that a company can generate reliable cash flow. It also requires deliberate capital allocation planning by management to make it a core tenet of the business. Consistent dividend growth typically indicates that a company is generating reliable cash flow, which in turn enables it to offer investors a combination of income and capital appreciation.

From the broader universe of investable stocks, I focus on those with a proven track record of annual dividend increases. I watch dividend increase announcements and combine those with other supporting data points. This information is core to my investment process, and I share it here with the Seeking Alpha community to help you make more informed investment decisions.

This week, I'll spotlight another group of companies and provide more detail on both the selections and the methodology behind them.

A few highlights from this week's list:

  • Average dividend increase: 7.8% (median: 7.6%)

  • Average dividend streak: 26 years (median: 19 years)

  • Dividend King Illinois Tool Works extends its 62-year streak with a 7.3% increase.

My strategy focuses on buying, holding, and adding to positions in companies that raise their dividends annually and have the potential to outperform relevant benchmarks over time.

How I Created The Lists

The information presented here is the result of combining multiple data sources: the "U.S. Dividend Champions" spreadsheet provides the universe of companies I review, and then upcoming dividend announcements. This combination joins data on companies with a track record of consistent dividend growth and the timeliness of their dividend increases. It's important to note that every company on this list has a minimum of 5 years of dividend growth history.

To be considered for this list, a company must offer higher total annual dividends. Although a company might not increase its dividend every calendar year, its overall annual dividend can still grow.

What Is The Ex-Dividend Date?

The ex-dividend date is when you must own shares to qualify for an upcoming dividend or distribution. To be eligible, you must have bought the shares by the end of the preceding business day. For instance, if the ex-dividend date is Tuesday, you must have acquired the shares by the market close on Monday. If the ex-dividend date falls on a Monday (or a Tuesday following a holiday on Monday), you must have purchased the shares by the previous Friday.

Dividend Streak Categories

Here are the definitions of the streak categories that I'll use throughout the piece.

  • King: 50+ years.
  • Champion/Aristocrat: 25+ years.
  • Contender: 10-24 years.
  • Challenger: 5+ years.
Category Count
King 1
Champion 1
Contender 4
Challenger 0

The Dividend Increases List

The data is sorted by the ex-dividend date (ascending) and then by the streak (descending):

Name Ticker Streak Forward Yield Ex-Div Date Increase Percent Streak Category
Illinois Tool Works Inc. (ITW) 62 2.48 30-Sep-25 7.33% King
BancFirst Corporation (BANF) 32 1.5 30-Sep-25 6.52% Champion
Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB) 15 3.54 30-Sep-25 8.11% Contender
Mondelez International, Inc. - Class A Common Stock (MDLZ) 12 3.1 30-Sep-25 6.38% Contender
State Street Corporation (STT) 15 2.97 1-Oct-25 10.53% Contender
Republic Services, Inc. (RSG) 22 1.1 2-Oct-25 7.76% Contender

Field Definitions

Streak: Years of dividend growth history are sourced from the U.S. Dividend Champions spreadsheet.

Forward Yield: The payout rate is calculated by dividing the new payout rate by the current share price.

Ex-Dividend Date: This is the date by which you must own the stock to receive the dividend.

Increase Percent: The percent increase.

Streak Category: This is the company's overall dividend history classification.

Show Me The Money

Here is a table that shows the new and old rates and the percentage increase. The table is sorted by ex-dividend day in ascending order and dividend streak in descending order.

Ticker Old Rate New Rate Increase Percent
ITW 1.5 1.61 7.33%
BANF 0.46 0.49 6.52%
FITB 0.37 0.4 8.11%
MDLZ 0.47 0.5 6.38%
STT 0.76 0.84 10.53%
RSG 0.58 0.63 7.76%

Additional Metrics

Here are additional metrics related to these companies. Some data points include yearly pricing action and the P/E ratio. The table is sorted the same way as the table above.

Ticker Current Price 52 Week Low 52 Week High PE Ratio % Off Low % Off High
ITW 260.1 214.66 279.13 30.76 21% Off Low 7% Off High
BANF 130.84 97.02 138.77 18.69 35% Off Low 6% Off High
FITB 45.14 32.25 49.07 13.67 40% Off Low 8% Off High
MDLZ 64.43 53.95 75.11 26.86 19% Off Low 14% Off High
STT 113.1 72.81 116.37 12.12 55% Off Low 3% Off High
RSG 228.08 197.61 258.75 29.73 15% Off Low 12% Off High

Tickers By Yield And Growth Rates

I've organized the table in descending order, allowing investors to prioritize the current yield. Additionally, the table includes some historical dividend growth rates. I've also incorporated the "Chowder Rule," the sum of the current yield and the five-year dividend growth rate.

Ticker Yield 1 Yr DG 3 Yr DG 5 Yr DG 10 Yr DG Chowder Rule
FITB 3.54 5.7 7.3 7.7 11 10.9
MDLZ 3.1 10.6 10.3 10.5 12.1 13.7
STT 2.97 43.5 9.2 7.4 9.1 10.4
ITW 2.48 7.2 7.1 7 12 9.3
BANF 1.5 7 8.5 7.5 10.5 8.9
RSG 1.1 41.8 7.3 7 7.4 8

Historical Returns

My investment strategy focuses on identifying stocks that consistently outperform the market and grow their dividend payouts over time. For broad exposure to U.S. equity markets, excluding the REIT sector, I recommend the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD).

SCHD features strong historical performance, offers a yield that exceeds that of the S&P 500, and consistently delivers increasing dividends. With over $70 in assets, it's an incredibly popular dividend-growth ETF. The ten-year dividend growth rate is one of the four key factors that SCHD tracks in its index.

Based on this analysis and further due diligence, I have included several companies in my portfolio and will sometimes make timely purchases of existing holdings. The charts below assume dividends are reinvested.

Our benchmark SCHD returned 215% over the past decade. As I mentioned above, I lose interest in individual holdings if they can't at least match that. Right off the bat, STT and MDLZ are not attractive to me with their 120% and 82% returns, respectively.

Now, the other four individual companies have all outperformed SCHD, which is what I'll now focus on.

FITB returned 232%, ITW 295%, BANF 415%, and our big winner this week is RSG with a total return of 551% over the past ten years.

Next Steps

I'm very selective about the individual holdings in my portfolio. The individual sleeve portion of my portfolio has 33 equal-weight holdings. Since that sleeve already accounts for a percentage of my overall portfolio, there are definitely diminishing returns to holding any additional assets.

I'm looking for companies that offer significant alpha over SCHD and stand out from it. Furthermore, I believe in the importance of consistent, strong earnings and dividend growth over time. These articles provide me (and I hope you) a chance to review what's performed well and to take a closer look at my current holdings.

I would say I'm interested in RSG, but I already own Waste Management (WM). Those two stocks trade very similarly, so there's no additional benefit of adding another one. But there are definitely some interesting performers on the list this week.

I'd love to hear your thoughts on my strategy, so feel free to share yours in the comments below! As always, do your due diligence before making any investment decisions.

data.title: Global Medical REIT: Why I Skipped The Commons And 7.4% Yielding Preferreds
data.description:
Global Medical REIT (NYSE:GMRE) faces heavy near-term refinancing liabilities that will place pressure on its funds from operations ("FFO") and could work to dampen its dividend coverage. This renders a position in the common shares as a tougher sell, even with an elevated dividend yield. GMRE focuses on healthcare facilities leased to physician groups and regional and national healthcare systems. The REIT engineered a one-for-five reverse stock split last month, which is important to note for the adjustment of the per-share metrics. Essentially, multiply the pre-reverse stock split metric by 5. GMRE last reported a quarterly cash dividend of $0.75 per share, around $0.15 per share pre-reverse stock split. The figure annualizes at $3 per share for an 8.9% dividend yield. GMRE had previously cut the payout by 29% in May, breaking away from what had been stable distributions since 2016. The REIT also has publicly traded preferred shares; the 7.50% Series A Cumulative Redeemable Preferreds (NYSE:GMRE.PR.A), which pays out a $1.88 annual coupon for a 7.4% dividend yield. Which is the better buy? Likely the common shares, but I'm not a fan of either security.

Firstly, the preferreds are trading above their $25 per share liquidation value by around 1.88%. While this isn't a material divergence, my investment style tilts towards preferreds trading at deep discounts. This helps lock in a margin of safety and builds upside exposure in the event the negative divergence to liquidation value narrows. For example, my position in Highland Income Fund's Series A 5.375% Preferreds (HFRO.PR.A) was built when the preferreds were trading at a 38.7% discount to liquidation value. This discount has since slimmed to 32.7%, helping my portfolio lock in capital gains beyond the current dividend yield. GMRE's preferreds are also perpetual, and are trading past their September 2022 call date. These were issued back in 2017 when the Fed funds rate was much lower.

Why Was The Dividend Cut?

GMRE reported fiscal 2025 second-quarter revenue of $37.97 million, up 10.9% over its year-ago comp and a beat by $2.4 million on consensus estimates. This drove FFO of $1 per share for the second quarter, in line with consensus and flat from FFO of $1 per share a year ago. GMRE also realized first-quarter 2025 FFO of $1 per share. The REIT's dividend coverage currently stands at 133%, or a roughly 75% payout ratio. This is well below the 90% requirement for a REIT to pay out, providing GMRE with more headroom following the cut. Further, the REIT is guiding for full-year fiscal 2025 FFO of $4.45 to $4.65 per share, meaning 152% coverage of its annualized dividend of $3 per share at the midpoint.

The cut made sense because GMRE was previously paying out a dividend that was more than it earned in FFO. The current relative depth of coverage does dampen the need for further cuts, but the REIT's constrained cash position does provide a reason for pause. GMRE's cash and cash equivalents ended the second quarter at $9.23 million, up from $7.8 million a year ago but still extremely underweighted against total debt of around $700 million. GMRE currently does not have to cut the dividend again, but with a weighted average interest rate of 4.09% on its debt and a term of 1.6 years, the REIT will be in a difficult position where it will have to refinance its debt at higher rates. This will likely be at a rate between the dividend yield on the common shares and the current weighted average interest rate on its debt. The REIT's weighted average rate on debt was up from 3.75% and a term of 2.0 years as of the start of its fiscal 2025.

Conclusion

GMRE's rental revenue was derived from 319 tenants with leases across 193 buildings, and no single tenant drove more than 10% of revenue. While this a positive for the REIT's risk profile, the extremely short-term maturity of its still cheap debt presents a significant barrier to taking a position in the commons. When aggregated with the recent dividend cut, the lack of progress on FFO growth, and the constrained cash position, GMRE does not currently present a compelling buy. Near-term Fed rate cuts will act to help lift sentiment on REIT's but even the most optimistic backdrop for rate cuts has 50 basis points more of cuts to end 2025 as the base scenario, keeping the Fed funds rate still relatively elevated ahead of GMRE's wall of refinancing. The ticker is a hold.

data.title: Circle: Poised To Thrive Even As Interest Rates Decline
data.description:
As the stock market falls from recent highs, one thought pervades my opportunistic mindset: which stocks, particularly momentum and growth stocks, have fallen sharper than their fundamentals might justify? What rebound opportunities exist in the crash?

While in general I regard most crypto and blockchain highfliers with skepticism, Circle Internet Group (NYSE:CRCL) caught my eye recently. The sponsor behind the number-two stablecoin, USDC, has now fallen well below its original $130 IPO price; despite one point reaching above $200 per share. As the stock recedes, the core question on investors’ minds is: can Circle pull off a rebound?

I’m initiating Circle at a buy rating. To address the elephant in the room: the core reason Circle has been falling recently is mostly the same reason other stocks are rising: lower interest rate expectations. Since this hurts Circle’s principal source of revenue (interest income), investors are rightly nervous. And yet I also think the sheer growth of USDC can’t be ignored. Use this dip as a buying opportunity.

The U.S. leader in regulated stablecoins

First, let’s start with what exactly Circle does. Circle is the sponsor behind one of the largest and best known stablecoins, USDC. For those new to the term, a stablecoin is a cryptocurrency that is pegged to the value of an underlying asset: in this case, 1:1 against the U.S. dollar. One of the best ways to think about stablecoins is fulfilling the original promise of crypto: by acting as an efficient means of exchange on the internet, minus the volatility and price fluctuation that has accompanied the speculative activity of other coins. Since there is no scarcity (like Bitcoin) and each USDC is backed by a real U.S. dollar held in reserve, the price does not move.

It’s probably not necessary to state that the stablecoin market is growing rapidly. As of the end of Q2, Circle noted that the market cap of stablecoins in circulation grew at a stunning 50% y/y clip to $223 billion. Transactions, meanwhile, grew at an even faster 75% y/y clip as more applications and merchants leverage blockchain technology to process payments. Stablecoins recently grew to more than 1% of U.S. M2 money supply.

The stablecoin industry as a whole saw a massive leap in adoption when the GENIUS Act was passed earlier this year by President Trump. GENIUS provided the first regulatory framework for oversight of stablecoins, including auditing reserve balances. Rather than strangle the industry with regulations, this was broadly seen as a positive for mainstream adoption.

USDC is only the second largest coin: USDT, or Tether, predates USDC and is roughly 2x larger. And yet regulation is the number one differentiator for USDC. Circle has taken a compliance first attitude toward USDC, while Tether, which is sponsored by a company domiciled in the British Virgin Islands, is less subject to regulation.

To me, and to many market observers, the regulatory safety net underpinning USDC is a major reason why Circle is poised to gain market share. The chart below showcases that USDC's share of crypto trading volumes has grown to 10%, up from just 1% in the year-ago quarter. This is just a beginning indication of the potential that USDC has to grow.

Key to USDC's market share expansion is a partnership with Binance, which is the largest crypto trading platform in the world. Since December 2024, Binance has made USDC more broadly available to its 240 million global customers. Since the partnership kicked off, USDC has also grown as a share of assets held in custody by Binance, as shown in the chart below:

Interest income: at risk as rates fall, but circulation growth should offset

Now let's turn our attention to the number-one factor that is pushing Circle stock down. Circle generates the lion's share of its revenue from interest held on the cash and short-term securities that back its issuance of USDC. As shown in the chart below, reserve returns have been falling alongside interest rate declines: in Q2, reserve returns of 4.14% (roughly matching current yields on U.S. short term treasury bonds, as measured by the SHV ETF (SHV)) fell -103 bps y/y.

After the close of the quarter, the Fed cut rates by a quarter point, and is expecting two further cuts by year-end. Current estimates are placing rate-cut odds for only one further cut in 2026. So all else equal, rates could fall by 100 bps relative to current levels, which would cut out ~25% of Circle's revenue stream.

Note as well that Circle still pays out a large portion of this revenue as "distribution fees" to its large crypto wallet partners, namely Coinbase and Binance. As rates have fallen and as Circle has focused on growth, its RLDC margin (revenue less distribution cost) has also dwindled somewhat to 38%, meaning that Circle only generates 38% of clean net revenue for each dollar it earns in interest income.

While I agree that interest rate declines are a headwind for Circle, there are two reasons that I think the company will still be able to pull ahead:

  • Circulation growth is far outpacing expected interest rate declines. As I previously mentioned, a ~100 bps fall in interest rates will hurt interest revenue by ~25%, relative to the company's current ~4% reserve return rate. But at the same time, USDC circulation grew at 90% y/y in the most recent quarter. I also think that the recent risk-off attitude in the stock market and the selloff in momentum stocks will push more investors to increase their allocation to cash - and USDC is now a core alternative for cash holdings. Coinbase currently pays a 4.1% yield on USDC held on its platform. All in all, Circle is still well poised for revenue growth as stablecoin participation and usage itself grows, and as Circle's own market share expands.
  • Alternative revenue streams. Circle recently released the Arc blockchain, which is its own payment protocol that is natively designed to support USDC transactions (as an alternative to using USDC on other blockchains, such as Ethereum). Circle can earn "gas" fees from transactions on the Arc network, which is a revenue stream that didn't exist entirely in 2024. While it's still early days and we have yet to see how wide of an adoption that Arc can achieve, this initiative helps to boost Circle's horizon beyond earning just interest alone.

Healthy adjusted EBITDA margin expansion and reasonable valuation

We also think that amid top-line concerns, Circle's growing profitability shouldn't be ignored. In Q2, Circle grew adjusted EBITDA at a 52% y/y pace (again, this is in spite of a 103bps y/y decline in reserve return rates) to $126 million, representing a 50% margin against revenue less distribution and transaction costs (the 38% RLDC margin we discussed earlier). This improved 5 points y/y.

Circle is guiding to $475-$490 million in full-year adjusted opex (excluding stock comp), which implies quarterly opex holding stable at ~$125 million per quarter through the balance of the year.

The company has also guided to a 40% CAGR in USDC circulation (which should more than offset interest rate pressure), growth in "other revenue" including the new Arc blockchain initiative, and RLDC margins stabilizing in the 36-38% range. To me, this is a powerful combination for further adjusted EBITDA margin expansion.

At face value, Circle looks expensive at a 58x forward adjusted EBITDA that sits slightly above 2x of Coinbase's valuation.

But the reason I think Circle's valuation is quite reasonable is because of its rapid pace of current adjusted EBITDA expansion (with all the factors necessary for continued growth still in place: USDC circulation growth well in excess of interest rate declines, modest opex growth, and stable distribution costs) which makes near-term adjusted EBITDA multiples less reliable.

Coinbase, on the other hand, looks cheap relative to Circle: but the vast majority of Coinbase's revenue depends on trading revenue, which is very volatile and fluctuates widely from quarter to quarter, whereas Circle's revenue is generated from a very stable interest income stream. In fact, Coinbase's revenue essentially flatlined in the most recent quarter (a function of lower crypto trading volatility) while adjusted EBITDA declined -14% y/y.

Key takeaways

I'd much rather bank on a stable, predictable company like Circle rather than bet on Coinbase's very volatile trading revenue. In my view, the tailwinds from the passage of the GENIUS act this year is unlocking substantial adoption of stablecoins and, in particular, USDC, which is positioning Circle for tremendous revenue growth in spite of interest rate headwinds. Interest rate risk is already priced into the stock: stay long and use the dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Choice Hotels International: Shares Look Cheap Amid Soft Domestic Demand
data.description:
Shares of Choice Hotels International (NYSE:CHH) have been a poor performer so far this year, declining by over 20%. While several of its peers have also not had the best of times recently, Choice's geographic and category mix have arguably left it particularly exposed to the prevailing macro environment in the United States. As a result, the Comfort Inn owner has been one of the weaker performing hotel stocks this year, while its shares have also trailed the S&P 500 by a wide margin.

While it may have hit a cyclical soft spot, there is still a lot to like about Choice's largely franchised business model. With only modest investment required to bring new rooms online, cash generation can largely be spent on things like share buybacks instead - something which is supporting the company's bottom line at the moment. Given that, Choice looks like a decent value play right now, with its shares only trading for around 15x consensus EPS after their year-to-date decline. Accordingly, I open on the name with a "Buy" rating.

A Heavily Franchised System

Broadly speaking, there are three models of hotel ownership: company-owned/leased, franchised, and managed. The company-owned/leased model should be fairly self-explanatory. These would be properties that Choice either owns outright or leases, leaving it responsible for things like construction, maintenance, capital expenditure and day-to-day operating expenses. Under this model, hotel-level profitability would obviously be the main driver of company-wide earnings.

The franchised model also requires little explanation. These hotels would be owned by another party, who would pay Choice a royalty fee to use one of its brands and the advantages that come with this (a pooled advertising fund, for example). This royalty fee would typically be a small cut of room revenue (~5%).

The third way - managed - may be the least intuitive. Under this model, a company like Choice would manage the hotel on behalf of its owners, who would then reimburse its costs. In exchange, Choice would receive a base fee, which would also be a cut of room revenue, as well as a share of the hotel's earnings.

Both franchised and managed hotels are asset light (in that Choice would not be responsible for things like construction or maintenance capital expenditures), but the latter tends to produce slightly more cyclical revenue, simply because hotel-level profitability will vary more than room revenue.

Choice's system consists of just under 650,000 rooms spread across 22 brands. Nearly all of these are in franchised hotels. Ultimately, this means that Choice's fee revenue and earnings will really be driven by system-wide room revenue. This will largely be a function of three things: the revenue per available room ('RevPAR'), the number of rooms in its system, and the royalty rate. In turn, RevPAR can be broken down into the rate per room and the hotel occupancy rate.

A Cyclical Soft Patch

Like its peers, elements of this algorithm have been pretty weak so far this year, specifically RevPAR in the United States, which fell by 2.9% last quarter and 0.7% over the first half of the year. As a result of this weakness, management has downgraded full-year U.S. RevPAR guidance twice so far this year, with the mid-point of its range now pointing to a 1.5% decline.

Over 75% of Choices rooms are located in the home market, so trends there are crucial. On the last earnings call, management was actually pretty bullish on the health of the U.S. consumer in general. Instead, it cited a drop in inbound international travel and government-related travel as reasons for the decline. The good news, if you can call it that, is that both of these are really macro-related issues rather than something to do with Choice. Indeed, peers like Hyatt (H), Hilton (HLT), Marriott (MAR) and Wyndham (WH) have all downgraded their 2025 RevPAR outlooks as well.

It's true that Choice's RevPAR outlook is the weakest of the bunch, but that's just a function of its heavy U.S. exposure, with peers able to lean on more resilient trends abroad. In addition, Choice's category mix skews heavily to midscale brands. It has no exposure to the luxury segment, where spending seems to have been more resilient based on the results of its peers.

System And Royalty Rate Still Expanding

While its RevPAR growth has hit a cyclical soft patch, other drivers of Choice's growth are still contributing positively. Most obviously, it is continuing to add new rooms to its system, which grew 2% year-on-year last quarter to 644,400 rooms. Choice has 93,000 rooms in its pipeline. While that is small next to peers, it can still sustain low single-digit growth for around 5-6 years.

The firm's royalty rate also continues to creep up. As Choice continues to demonstrate the value of its brands to franchise partners, it has been able to command higher rates on new contracts. As a result, its pipeline has a higher effective royalty rate than its current system does. As new rooms continue to come online, this will continue to drive the rate higher over time, as has happened historically:

There is another mix effect to consider. Above, I said that Choice lacked meaningful exposure to brands in higher price points. Management is addressing this, and brands in the upscale category now account for around 30% of the company's room pipeline. As the room mix gradually shifts, it should be a tailwind for firm-wide RevPAR, albeit this is currently being masked by this line's cyclical weakness.

As a result of these trends, fee revenue has held up better than RevPAR this year, clocking in roughly flat in H1 at $322 million.

Margin Expansion, Capital Returns Drive Earnings Growth

Another reason to like fee-heavy businesses like Choice is that their own operating expenses don't need to rise at the same rate as fee growth. This can drive profit margin expansion over time, meaning earnings can grow faster than revenue. We can see that in the company's recent results. Whereas Choice's total revenue fell 1% in the first half to $759 million, operating expenses declined by over 3%. Adjusted EBITDA rose 3% to $295 million.

Then there are the capital returns to consider. Because franchised hotels are so asset light, Choice doesn't need to investment much cash to grow its system. As such, it tends to spend heavily on shareholder returns, which is mainly conducted through share buybacks.

As you can seen, Choice has lowered its share count by over 15% over the past three years, and this has supported its per-share earnings growth. Choice made $3.25 per share in adjusted net income over the first half of the year. Despite its soft RevPAR, this was up around 5% year-on-year.

The Valuation

Choice stock trades for around $109 as I type, equal to just 15.3x 2025 consensus EPS and 14.8x 2026 consensus. Like I said above, Choice's pipeline is on the smaller side than peers, so it won't grow its earnings as fast as they will. However, that is reflected in its valuation, which is also lower than peers, as shown below.

Choice's price-to-earnings ratio also looks cheap compared to its past levels. Based on its year-end price, the stock has typically traded for around 20-21x adjusted diluted EPS (I've excluded 2020 and 2021, given the impact that COVID had on its earnings). That would put its current multiple at a circa 20% discount.

Between RevPAR, room count growth, royalty rate expansion and share buybacks, Choice has solid prospects for long-term EPS growth. It also pays a 1% dividend. And with its valuation looking historically cheap, today's buyer may even some multiple expansion as well. I think that can comfortably support a double-digit annualized return. Accordingly, I rate the stock a "Buy".

data.title: Northern Dynasty Minerals: Receives Another $12 Million Investment While Preparing For The Court Case
data.description:
Northern Dynasty Minerals (NYSE:NAK) continues to have discussions with the US government around withdrawing the veto, but it seems likely that the issue will be settled in the courts. The opening briefs in the case are due by October 3, 2025, and then the Department of Justice's [DOJ] response brief is due by January 2, 2026. There is no timeline around a potential ruling yet, but mid-2026 appears to be a reasonable expectation there.

Northern Dynasty received some positive news with the receipt of the fourth US$12 million tranche investment, giving it additional liquidity. This is a relatively minor item in terms of impact compared to the legal proceedings, though.

I remain neutral on Northern Dynasty as a near-term trading stock at its current price of a bit over US$1 per share.

This report uses US dollars as its main currency, along with an exchange rate of US$1.00 to CAD$1.39.

Legal Timeline

The summary judgment briefing schedule has been set in the case, with opening briefs due by October 3. The DOJ's response brief is due by January 2, 2026, and then the plaintiffs (the State of Alaska, Pebble Partnership and Iliamna Natives Ltd, et al.) have their response to the DOJ's briefs due by February 27, 2026.

In general, I'd expect Judge Gleason to issue her ruling in mid-2026, although there is a significant amount of historical variation around how long it takes judges to issue their rulings.

Northern Dynasty is also continuing to have discussions with the US government around withdrawing the veto. If the discussions are fruitful, then Northern Dynasty would terminate the summary judgment process as it would have no need for that anymore.

However, I expect that this will need to get resolved through the courts given the lack of progress through the discussions to date.

Another Tranche Investment

Northern Dynasty received the fourth US$12 million tranche investment under its July 2022 royalty agreement a bit ahead of the September 30, 2025 deadline for that tranche. The royalty investor now has until the end of 2025 to decide on whether they will make the fifth (and last) US$12 million tranche payment under the agreement.

So far the royalty investor has invested US$48 million for the right to purchase 8% of Pebble's payable gold production and 24% of its payable silver production. The royalty investor will pay US$1,500 per ounce of gold and US$10 per ounce of silver under the deal, although Northern Dynasty also gets 20% of any excess price if gold is over US$4,000 or silver is over US$50.

So if Pebble starts producing at some point in the future and gold is US$5,000 per ounce, the royalty investor would pay US$1,700 per ounce for their gold.

Northern Dynasty now has a solid amount of cash on hand. It reported CAD$25.2 million (US$18.1 million) in cash and cash equivalents at the end of Q2 2025.

Northern Dynasty's cash position is now over US$30 million pro forma for this tranche investment. And it also noted that it also received additional money from the exercise of options and warrants (likely when its stock was going up in early July).

Northern Dynasty should now have enough cash to cover operations until the end of 2027 based on its recent burn rate. If the royalty holder makes the final US$12 million tranche investment, that would give Northern Dynasty enough cash to cover operations until the end of 2028.

I had previously thought that the fourth and fifth tranche investments were uncertain if the veto negotiations didn't work out. However, the royalty investor appears to be inclined to make the investments despite the case going to court.

From the royalty investor's perspective, the investment may be seen as a long-shot bet with potentially a very high payout. If Pebble enters production, the US$60 million royalty investment (assuming the last tranche investment is made) could eventually result in a profit of billions for the royalty investor.

Notes On Valuation

I am keeping a near-term neutral outlook on Northern Dynasty as a trading stock with its share price at a bit over US$1. The additional tranche investment is a positive for it, giving it additional cash to cover operations for another year (until around the end of 2027 now).

Pebble does have a massive amount of potential value if it ever reaches production, especially given the general upward trend in metal prices. However, there also remain multiple large hurdles to becoming a producing mine, with the current court case just one of those hurdles.

Conclusion

Northern Dynasty received a bit of positive news with the additional US$12 million tranche investment. I am also assuming that the final US$12 million tranche investment is likely to be made too, giving Northern Dynasty several years of runway.

The court case is proceeding, with opening briefs due in a week. Based on the published timeline, I'd generally expect Judge Gleason's decision by mid-2026.

Pebble remains a potentially very valuable asset with multiple large obstacles in the way of it becoming an operating mine. Northern Dynasty functions more as a trading stock that reacts to news coming out. The end of 2025 decision on the final US$12 million investment is a minor future event, while Judge Gleason's decision will have a much larger impact on Northern Dynasty's share price.

data.title: BigBear.ai: When Price Leads The Story: Why This Breakout Matters (And What Must Happen Next)
data.description:
If you’re looking at BigBear.ai (NYSE:BBAI) right now and thinking, “the stock shouldn’t be ripping like this after that earnings mess,” you’re not alone. The tape is telling one story while the near-term fundamentals still tell another. This is one of those moments where price is potentially running ahead of the narrative, and the question is whether the narrative can catch up before the chart exhausts.

In plain English, we got a classic post-dump base, a gap reclaim, and a momentum flip in a name that still has real execution questions. That can be powerful, and treacherous, depending on your time horizon and risk tolerance.

Quick backdrop: How we got here

Q2 was a gut punch. Management cut full-year revenue guidance to $125–$140 million, withdrew adjusted EBITDA guidance entirely, and flagged disruptions on U.S. Army programs as that customer consolidates and modernizes its data architecture.

Reported Q2 revenue fell to $32.5 million (down ~18% year over year), gross margin slipped to about 25% (vs. ~27.8% a year ago), adjusted EBITDA deteriorated to -$8.5 million (from -$3.7 million), and the P&L was crushed by non-cash items: a ~$136 million derivative revaluation loss and a ~$71 million goodwill impairment tied in part to the lower outlook.

The only unequivocal “win” in the quarter was the balance sheet: the company raised roughly $293 million via its at-the-market facility (about 75 million shares sold at an average of $3.90), finishing with ~$391 million of cash and, for the first time, a meaningful net cash position.

If you’re a short-term trader, that quarter screamed, “fundamentals deteriorating, valuation needs to compress.” If you’re a long-term strategist, you saw “fortress liquidity, optionality to play offense in the biggest funding wave defense AI has ever seen.”

Enter the technical set-up

After the earnings gap-down, BBAI built a tight base in the $6s–$7s, then reclaimed the entire post-earnings gap zone (~$6.9–$7.8) and flipped it to support. That matters because old supply became new demand; every seller in that gap is now underwater and less willing to dump at former levels.

Momentum confirms the change in character: RSI shifted from sub-40 to a strong 70–80 regime (that isn’t “just overbought” in a young uptrend; it’s gas), and MACD curled up through the zero line, the point where a lot of trend systems add exposure.

Volume expanded on up days. Put it together and you have the textbook ingredients of short-covering + momentum funds pressing the long side after a flush. The market doesn’t need a press release for that; the chart is enough.

The line in the sand is the prior swing high. In BBAI’s case, the “boss level” sits around $9.2–$9.6. Why that zone? Because it’s the last swing high before the earnings breakdown, the level that defines whether the stock is still in a damage-control range or transitioning back into an intermediate uptrend.

Clearing it with authority (think 1.8–2.5× average daily volume and a daily close above the zone) does three mechanical things: it creates a supply vacuum (not many trapped longs above to sell), it triggers systematic trend/momentum flows that require new highs on volume, and it can add gamma fuel if there’s a concentration of calls around $9/$10 forcing dealers to hedge by buying shares. In other words, a clean break is a signal that price is leading the story, and that you might get a second leg higher even without a new fundamental catalyst in hand.

Why this “price first, narrative later” dynamic is plausible here. The long-term story, the one management is trying to sell, is legitimately massive: the so-called “OB3” supplemental funding wave that allocates ~$170B to DHS and ~$150B to DoD, with explicit buckets for border technology, biometric exit, AI autonomy, and shipbuilding — all lanes BBAI already plays in with veriScan/Trueface (biometrics), ConductorOS (autonomy enablement), and Shipyard.ai (industrial base, scheduling, and optimization).

International partnerships (UAE’s IHC umbrella, Panama’s Narval cargo security) broaden the surface area further. The balance sheet is a weapon: ~$391M in cash means BBAI can fund capture campaigns, accelerate deployments, and even pursue transformational M&A without tapping expensive debt.

On paper, if they convert even a sliver of those OB3-aligned opportunities into funded, multiyear awards that start soon, the revenue slope can steepen from low single-digit to something investors can actually model. The issue is timing, and that’s precisely why the chart can get ahead of the story in the interim.

But let’s be real about the near-term fundamentals

Management admitted the pipeline was narrow and reliant on a few large contracts; they’re working to broaden it, but that takes time. They also made it clear they’re going on offense: more spend on marketing, talent, product, and partnerships — and no EBITDA guide while they invest — which is code for lower earnings before they’re higher.

The equity raise was dilutive in the here and now (that’s ~25%+ more shares), even if it unlocked the cash they needed. At the operating level, revenue contracted, gross margin compressed, and the Army program disruption will take time to re-compete or replace. None of that screams “imminent P&L inflection.”

So why entertain the breakout at all? Because the tape says buyers are willing to front-run the possibility that backlog and award flow improve before the Street believes it. This is where “leading indicators” matter. In a name like BBAI, revenue, margins, and EBITDA are lagging.

The leading indicator is backlog (especially funded backlog) and how fast it converts. We’ve already seen backlog grow from the mid-$300Ms to over $400M earlier this year; if that metric inflects again with funded awards tied to OB3 buckets — think CBP biometric exit and primary processing, AI autonomy (ConductorOS and distributed systems), shipbuilding/digital thread — and if the period of performance starts in the next quarter or two, then the Street will rerate forward revenue growth and the chart will have been right to sprint. Until then, this is technicals doing the heavy lifting.

Valuation in context

You can’t ignore the comp set. Compared with old-school federal integrators like Booz Allen (BAH) or Leidos (LDOS) that trade ~1–2× sales with mature cash flow, BBAI’s multiple has hovered well above that because it’s pitching platform-like upside in defense AI. Compared with “AI platform” peers like C3.ai (AI) or the mission-software behemoth Palantir (PLTR), BBAI is tiny, still negative on EBITDA, and far more contract-lumpy.

The board and the market both “priced in” optionality earlier this year; the guidance cut forced a reset. For this break to stick, the company must show the path from cash → capture → funded backlog → revenue conversion → margin stabilization. Without that, any multiple expansion from a breakout risks being temporary, the kind algos sell into when headlines go quiet.

Risk management (for traders and investors)

So, what to do with BBAI here? If you’re here for the trade, you have a clean heuristic: above $9.6 on volume and holds → the path of least resistance is up; below $7.6 on rising down-volume → the move’s probably a head-fake and the base needs more time.

If you’re here for the investment, your DPIs are backlog composition (funded multiyear wins), conversion pace, gross margin trajectory, and any verified OB3-aligned awards with near-term start dates. The balance sheet gives them time; the pipeline needs breadth; the execution needs to show up in numbers. Yes, this can be price leading the story for a stretch — that’s how big winners start — but the story still has to show up.

If you’re trading the chart, the playbook is straightforward: you want a daily close above $9.6 on 1.8–2.5× average volume, a short flag/pause just above that zone, and then a retest of $9.2–$9.6 that holds. That’s your “price is leading the story” confirmation.

Stops belong just below the breakout shelf (call it $8.95–$9.10), or under an anchored VWAP from the breakout day, with a measured move toward $10.0–$10.4 based on the height of the $6–$8 base. If the stock fails to hold that shelf twice on expanding down-volume, respect the failed breakout and step aside; probability shifts to a longer base between $8.0–$8.6 or even a retest of the $7.6 gap shelf.

If you’re investing, position sizing needs to reflect that this is still a speculative, contract-driven story. The time horizon mismatch is real: technicals play out over days–weeks, while the fundamental payoff (OB3 capture, international deployments, M&A integration) is quarters.

What would make the move “fundamentally real.” There are three things that would validate price leading the story. First, funded backlog acceleration with start dates inside the next two quarters; this tells you not just that they “won” something, but that revenue recognition is imminent.

Second, proof of international monetization, i.e., announcements that UAE-linked deployments are live and paying, or that the Panama cargo security solution is scaling to big shipping lines. Third, stable to rising gross margin back toward the high 20s/low 30s as software/IP-heavy projects mix up - confirmation they’re not simply growing services headcount to chase revenue. Any combination of those three, while the chart is above $9.2–$9.6, is the recipe for a sustained rerate.

What could break it. Two things will kill this breakout fast: a return to heavy ATM usage without related contract wins (share count up, narrative flat), and another guide-down or evidence that government award/obligation timing is pushing conversion further right. Also, keep an eye on Army re-competes; losing a high-profile one could reset expectations again, even if the broader OB3 wave is still out there.

A word on M&A because cash is burning a hole in their pocket. Management said the quiet part out loud: they are “actively pursuing strategic transformational acquisitions.” That can accelerate scale… or blow up value if they overpay or mis-integrate.

The “right” deal would add immediate revenue scale, complementary IP, and distribution in federal or Tier-1 international channels, with a clear line of sight to margins. The “wrong” deal would be a headcount-heavy services roll-up with thin gross profit and integration debt. If they announce an acquisition while the stock is breaking out, read what they bought before you assume it’s bullish - the mix matters.

Bottom line. BBAI is a classic asymmetric optionality case right now. The technicals argue for a legitimate momentum turn - gap reclaim, rising volume, RSI regime shift, MACD above zero, and a very clear breakout level - while the fundamentals argue for patience: guidance reset, EBITDA uncertainty, dilution, and a multi-quarter capture/conversion slog.

Those two realities aren’t mutually exclusive. They intersect if (and only if) management converts the OB3 opportunity, diversifies the pipeline, and turns that $391M cash pile into revenue scale with margin.

Until then, trade the tape with discipline and size the investment like a speculative sleeve. If they start printing funded awards and backlog inflects while the stock holds above $9.2–$9.6, the tape will have been right to front-run; if not, respect your levels and don’t marry the ticker.

Disclaimer: No position. This text expresses the views of the author as of the date indicated, and such views are subject to change without notice. The author has no duty or obligation to update the information contained herein. Further, wherever there is the potential for profit, there is also the possibility of loss. Additionally, the present article is being made available for educational purposes only and should not be used for any other purpose. The information contained herein does not constitute and should not be construed as an offering of advisory services or an offer to sell or solicitation to buy any securities or related financial instruments in any jurisdiction. Some information and data contained herein concerning economic trends and performance is based on or derived from information provided by independent third-party sources. The author trusts that the sources from which such information has been obtained are reliable; however, it cannot guarantee the accuracy of such information and has not independently verified the accuracy or completeness of such information or the assumptions on which such information is based

data.title: Inflation Still Over Target In August
data.description:
By William J. Luther

Inflation has exceeded the Fed’s two percent target for 54 consecutive months, with prices up 25 percent since March 2020.

Prices rose faster last month, according to new data from the Bureau of Economic Analysis. The Personal Consumption Expenditures Price Index (PCEPI), which is the Federal Reserve’s preferred measure of inflation, grew at an annualized rate of 3.2 percent in August 2025, up from 2.0 percent in the prior month. It has averaged 2.2 percent over the last six months and 2.7 percent over the last year.

Core inflation, which excludes volatile food and energy prices but also places more weight on housing services prices, declined. According to the BEA, core PCEPI grew at an annualized rate of 2.8 percent in August 2025, down from 2.9 percent in July. It has averaged 2.5 percent over the last six months and 2.9 percent over the last year.

Inflation appears to be closer to the Fed’s two percent target when imputed - or, estimated - prices are excluded. Some prices, like those for owner-occupied housing and financial services furnished without payment (e.g., free checking accounts), are not directly observed and must be estimated for inclusion in the PCEPI.

But there is some debate about the quality of such estimates. Market-based PCE, which is a supplemental measure offered by the BEA based on household expenditures for which there are observable prices, grew at an annualized rate of 2.5 percent in August 2025. It has averaged 2.0 percent over the last six months and 2.4 percent over the last year.

Market-based core PCE, which removes food and energy prices in addition to most imputed prices, grew 1.9 percent in August 2025. It has averaged 2.3 percent over the last six months and 2.6 percent over the last year.

Fed officials seem inclined to attribute the recent uptick in inflation to higher tariffs imposed by the Trump administration, something monetary policymakers should ignore. Last week, the Federal Open Market Committee voted to lower the federal funds rate target range by 25 basis points, marking the first cut since December 2024.

FOMC members also signaled that additional rate cuts would soon follow. The median FOMC member projected the midpoint of the federal funds rate target range would decline another 50 basis points this year. That approach is sensible if one expects tariffs will have a temporary effect on inflation and little-to-no effect on inflation expectations.

“Goods prices, after falling last year, are driving the pickup in inflation,” Fed Chair Jerome Powell said earlier this week. “Incoming data and surveys suggest that those price increases largely reflect higher tariffs rather than broader price pressures.” He noted that prices for services continued to grow more slowly.

Powell also indicated that inflation expectations appear to be well anchored. Although inflation expectations for the next year “have moved up, on balance, over the course of this year on news about tariffs,” Powell said, “most measures of longer-term expectations remain consistent with our two-percent inflation goal.”

At the same time, Fed officials seem worried that tight monetary policy may be causing the economy to grow more slowly than it should. Powell said the “moderation in growth” observed in the first half of 2025 “largely reflects a slowdown in consumer spending.” He also said the labor market had become “less dynamic and somewhat softer,” with higher downside risks to employment.

All of this suggests the Fed will proceed with its plans to cut rates further this year, despite higher inflation. The CME Group reports the implied odds of an October rate cut at 89.8 percent. It reports a 97.2 percent chance that the federal funds rate will be at least 25 basis points lower following the December meeting, and a 65.0 percent chance the federal funds rate will be 50 basis points lower.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Bloom Energy: The Not Too Overvalued Bet Fueling The Data Center CapEx Boom
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Sarfatti Investment Research as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction

I am initiating coverage on Bloom Energy (NYSE:BE) with a Strong Buy rating. BE is very well positioned in today’s market to deploy its fuel cell solutions to support the rapid data center CapEx expansion fueled by AI. Further exacerbating BE’s growth prospects is the current natural gas-forward energy mix in the US, with little propensity to change in the medium term. My thesis will be focused largely on data centers, as this is where growth is concentrated, with a brief aside on maritime developments.

Background

Before diving into BE, it is necessary to gain some context on the underlying industry supporting BE’s rapid expansion in the next years. Nebius (NBIS) recently announced a $17.4 billion deal, with the option to reach $19.4 billion, with Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) to provide compute through its New Jersey data center over the next 5 years. Furthermore, CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV) has over $30 billion in revenue backlog and contracts with big-ticket AI-centric customers like Microsoft, Mistral AI, OpenAI among others. In addition, Oracle (ORCL) has also signed a deal with OpenAI worth $300 billion to provide computing over 5 years starting from 2027.

The past few months have seen agreements, in addition to the recent ones mentioned above, worth hundreds of billions of dollars to gain access to compute power—with an innate property of being energy intensive. Additionally, many of these deals are resulting in greenfield data center development, with OpenAI's Stargate Project investing $500 billion into building new AI infrastructure in the US. Developing greenfield sites (fresh build-outs on undeveloped land) will allow developers to design data centers with the most energy-efficient and modern infrastructure.

Furthermore, McKinsey projects a +3.5x change in the total data center capacity demand for AI workloads alone. The expected capital expenditure to support this increase in demand is expected to be $5.2 trillion. McKinsey further segments this CapEx spend in another article with a $6.7 trillion scenario, implying a total spend on power generation worth $400 billion, or ~6% of overall CapEx spending.

Product Overview & Competitor Analysis

For this section, I am solely going to focus on BE’s solid oxide fuel cell (SOFC) offering and not touch their solid oxide electrolyzers (SOEC) because SOFCs are currently their flagship product, and they are going to be the key driver in continuing BE’s growth journey.

So, what are SOFCs? They are high-temperature ceramic fuel cells that electrochemically convert fuel to electricity, usually with high electrical efficiency (60%). These SOFCs reform hydrocarbons like natural gas, biogas, and syngas. The key value add with SOFCs is their high efficiency, compact form-factor, lower carbon footprint compared to incumbent solutions, and fuel flexibility. Within the fuel cell segment, SOFCs are most directly competing with proton exchange membrane (PEM) fuel cells. I compared them below. It is clear SOFCs are a moderately better choice for data centers given their generally higher efficiency and fuel flexibility but also easier availability of said fuels.

SMRs are being touted as the next big revolution for energy access in data centers. I don’t deny this, but SMRs are at a minimum 2+ years out from regulatory approval in an optimistic scenario, with some analyses suggesting mid to late 2030s for significant adoption. For a more near- to medium-term solution, SOFCs, in my opinion, are far more realistic.

It is also crucial to touch on the incumbent technology that these SOFCs are attempting to phase out—large sets of generators mostly made by companies like Caterpillar (CAT). CAT’s products are currently used within data centers for both primary and backup power. CAT’s overall energy and transportation business (which includes data centers but also other verticals) generated $28 billion in sales in 2024, accounting for about 44% of machinery-related sales.

So where does the value proposition of SOFCs over traditional generators come from? SOFCs cost more upfront, but result in a higher operational efficiency of about 30%, resulting in a net saving over usage lifecycles. SOFCs also have lower local emissions, a more compact form factor, faster time-to-market, and greater fuel flexibility, as previously mentioned. However, the key detracting factors include a less mature service footprint, a limited long-term track record, and a slower start in outages.

Thesis on US Energy Mix

Today, energy in the US is obtained largely through fossil fuels, with the two largest energy sources being petroleum and natural gas (used in the SOFCs).

Additionally, much of data center colocation growth, according to JLL, is concentrated towards low-cost electricity markets. In particular, states like Ohio, Texas, Virginia, Arizona, and Georgia are seeing data center development growth. This is largely driven by the fact that natural gas is abundant and cheap in the US. Most of the states where data center development is growing have a higher usage of natural gas than the national consumption rate of 36%, as shown below.

Data centers today are utilizing very high amounts of energy, consuming 4.4% of total electricity in the US in 2023, with estimates of up to 12% usage by 2028. According to the UCS, meeting this added demand through new, less fossil fuel-reliant sources requires more transmission lines, which can take 5-9 years to plan and build. This time horizon is too far out to meet current demands for compute. Data centers are left with no option but to utilize current energy infrastructure, currently heavily skewed towards natural gas. So, what does this mean for BE? SOFCs very efficiently utilize natural gas, and they propose a value proposition over traditional gensets, resulting in BE becoming very well positioned to realize this boom in data center CapEx.

Recent Developments

In July, BE announced a partnership with ORCL to power AI data centers within 90 days, piggybacking on ORCL’s $25 billion CapEx plan to expand AI infrastructure capabilities. BE also has aggressive plans to double manufacturing capacity to 2 GW by EoY 2025 by investing $100 million. American Electric Power Company, Inc. (AEP), one of BE’s current clients, is working on constructing a new natural gas pipeline to power a data center in Ohio, with an expansion order to BE expected soon. Additionally, on top of deals and announcements, BE is also able to execute 3 quarters in a row of record revenue and profits with $401 million in revenue in Q2 2025, a 19.5% increase quarter over quarter.

Additionally, BE will also have the capacity to diversify away from just data centers, with the American Bureau of Shipping approving Bloom Energy’s 65kW Marine Power Module on board ships. The International Maritime Organization, the UN agency responsible for developing and maintaining a comprehensive regulatory framework for shipping, will enforce sustainability measures for large ocean-going ships starting 2027 aiming for net-zero emissions by 2050. A.P. Møller - Mærsk A/S (OTCPK:AMKBY) also states that fuel cells could provide a reduction in certain emissions, further abetting a use-case of fuel cells to reduce emissions in maritime.

Risks

Although BE is seemingly well-positioned, there are multiple risks that would change my future outlook on the company. An execution risk exists, particularly with the ORCL deal, where bold claims of deployment within 90 days were made. Additionally, neither the size of the deal, nor a multi-year commitment was announced, signaling potential uncertainty on ORCL’s end and potentially unfavorable unit economics for BE. This risk is further intensified if BE is not able to materialize proposed cost reductions.

Another key risk dimension to consider is regulatory risk. Although BE’s SOFCs are more sustainable than incumbent solutions, they still face risk from evolving emissions regulations. This risk is amplified when considering BE operates internationally in geographies like the EU (notorious for strict sustainability regulations) and Japan.

Given BE operates in a very competitive market, new entrants & emerging technologies could pose cleaner & more energy-efficient solutions, which can also drive regulatory changes. Furthermore, industrial customers like factories & data centers could have charges imposed on them by regulators for grid defection by utilizing distributed generation—the practice of producing electricity at localized power sources as opposed to being connected to the centralized power grid. BE’s business model relies heavily on industrial customers choosing BE to decrease grid dependence.

Valuation

There has been lots of discussion on BE being overvalued. I present a contrarian view where I believe there is lots of room for growth for BE. In the above table is a list of companies comparable to BE and their corresponding multiples. I think that in today’s markets it is not sound to use LTM multiples for valuation, especially for growth companies like BE, where much of the revenue & EBITDA growth is concentrated towards the future. Many deals, such as the recent one with ORCL, are not factored into revenue yet, but are accounted for in share price.

Looking at NTM multiples, BE actually trades at a discount to similar high-growth companies with lots of room for future growth and deals, given the rapid expansion in data center CapEx. Yes, it can be argued that the companies I have selected are overvalued themselves, but many present a similar profile to BE, with NBIS having strong forward-looking momentum with their Microsoft deal and CRWV presenting a similar case with their backlog.

Above is a sensitivity table showing how implied share price changes under different assumptions for revenue multiples. The price target is derived by multiplying revenue by the target multiple to obtain enterprise value, which is then adjusted by net debt to arrive at equity value, which is then divided by total fully diluted shares outstanding to reach the share price. For my PT, I am using the median of TEV/Revenue NTM multiples from the previous table of 9.0x, resulting in a 12-month price target of $166. I am expecting margin expansion over the next quarters driven by investments in increasing manufacturing capacity to increase margins. I also expect further revenue growth driven by higher adoption of SOFCs in data centers, exacerbated by the current energy infrastructure situation in the US, which is not due to revolutionize in the medium-term.

Conclusion

To conclude, BE is well positioned to take advantage of the opportunity created by a strong increase in data center CapEx driven by the high compute demands of developing AI solutions. BE's SOFCs propose a strong value proposition over incumbent solutions, and any real threats like SMRs are multiple years out. Further strengthening BE's positioning is the natural gas forward energy infrastructure in the US. Although there are real risks like regulatory changes and failure to execute, I believe that BE has a strong growth journey ahead and offers investors an attractive entry point to one of the key enablers in fueling AI.

data.title: Apple: I Was Wrong In July; That's Why I'm Switching To Buy Today
data.description:
Mea culpa

During the journey of my career, I have gradually understood to be humble and to be aware of my mistakes and consequently admit them. Where I come from, they say, “If you don't work, you don't make mistakes,” but I don't mean to justify myself with that. As I mentioned, I completely miscalculated the situation with Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) in July (Apple Q3: $100 Billion In Buybacks, $9 Billion In CapEx - Why I'm Not Buying). I have always been a fan of the brand, both as a consumer of the products and services that it offers and as a long-term investor, but in July I was convinced that the stock would have to stay at a plateau, at least for a period.

I had worries regarding the overpriced assessment of the stock and the hints of deceleration and lack of visibility on the AI issue it was suffering from, which made me feel upset. Apple has been a lot more durable than I expected for the last two months, so at this point, I am changing my decision. I am now promoting Apple to a "buy" (in the long term). I will lay out a number of factors for this alteration of view, which I will expound on in this analysis.

Overview

The company certainly needs no introduction. It is one of the largest tech companies in the world, with a business dominated by a product that no one can do without anymore, namely the iPhone (about half of its revenue comes from this), even though over the years it has been able to diversify with great success. The services division has a gross margin of around 74%, almost double that of the hardware division (around 37%).

Category

Revenue 2024 ($, Billions)

 Share of Revenue

iPhone

 201 51%
Services 96 25%
Wearables, Home and Access 37 9%
Mac 30 8%
iPad 27 7%
Total Revenue 391 100%

From a financial point of view, it generates impressive cash flow. Every year, it generates tens of billions in free cash flow, most of which is returned to shareholders through a regular and aggressive buyback policy. Just to give you an idea, in the last decade alone, the company has repurchased over $900 billion worth of its own shares (see table below, source: Bloomberg Terminal) and, in the meantime, pays quarterly dividends (albeit modest ones of around 0.5%).

Why I was pessimistic in July

Stretched valuation and slowing growth

Usually, the first thing I look at is multiples, and for my taste, the stock's valuation was (and still is today) a bit stretched. On the other hand, it is also true that we are getting used to this new era in which high valuations seem almost the new normal for the markets. Be that as it may, the P/E ratio is currently 35x earnings, and EV/EBITDA is 25x, which is very high compared to the historical average. Normally, these values are fine for a fast-growing tech company, but less so for a company like Apple, which was beginning to show signs of slowing down.

Macro and geopolitical risks

The current macroeconomic context is not the best. Apple in particular is in a complicated situation with China because it is both a market where it sells (17% of its revenue comes from there) and an extremely important production base. My fear was that at a delicate time like this, with the two countries engaged in a trade war, Apple could be one of the targets. We have already seen something like this in the past. Remember 2023? The Communist Party banned the use of iPhones by all government employees and officials. Apple also has some problems in the West, particularly with the European Community, whose strict regulations seem to represent a difficult obstacle to overcome.

CapEx at a minimum, buybacks at a maximum

The heart of the matter? The main reason I recommended “sell” in my previous analysis is the capital management; in fact, in the last quarter, Apple spent $9.4 billion on capex and $22.9 billion on buybacks, which is almost triple its productive investments. For me, this aspect is really very important. We are in a market phase where the first to arrive wins (I'm talking about the AI race). I am very interested in this aspect and have already discussed it when analyzing Meta (Meta: $70 Billion Later, The Real AI Winner Is Here), Microsoft (Microsoft Q4 Preview: AI Is Not A Gamble, It's Already Helping Margins), and Alphabet (Alphabet Spends $85 Billion To Build The Future, Wall Street Panics - I Buy). Simply put, Meta spends over $40 billion on AI and data centers, Alphabet talks about a super spending cycle, and Microsoft is the main financier of OpenAI, but in reality, it is at the center of the whole mechanism.

So my question is: where is Apple? The world's most innovative company seems to me to be cornered, waiting to make its move. It seemed distracted by inflating EPS with buybacks but not securing its future by investing in AI.

What has changed since July?

Among the new positive catalysts, I would put the release of the new iPhone at the top of the list, which, compared to expectations, seems to be in high demand. According to Ming-Chi Kuo's analysis (for those who don't know him, he is a long-standing Apple expert), demand for the iPhone 17 seems to be robust, and I quote:

“Production of the iPhone 17, 17 Pro, and 17 Pro Max for the third quarter of this year is about 25% higher than their predecessors last year, along with increased pre-order demand for all three models.”

The absolute novelty of this generation is the iPhone Air, which, regardless of whether you like the design or not, is designed to offer a “full screen” experience at lower prices than the Pro. Apple has essentially added a fourth model to the lineup (17, 17 Pro, 17 Pro Max, and Air) to better cover various price ranges and embrace a wider range of consumers

  • The base model iPhone 17 now has features previously reserved for the Pro (such as 120Hz ProMotion and Always-On display, according to MacRumors).
  • The Pro models retain differences (materials, better cameras) that make them preferable to advanced users.
  • The new Air could win over those who want a large screen but don't need multiple cameras.

I like this strategy because it shows me how Apple is starting to pay more attention to maximizing ARPU (average selling price) by pushing the Pro models, but at the same time trying not to lose price-sensitive customers by offering the Air.

On the AI issue, however, I remain somewhat perplexed because, while it is true that something is happening, I still find it difficult to see it as a concrete lever in the short term. I consider it more as a sort of “call option,” so if it arrives, it will improve the current situation. I expect and hope that Apple will keep pace with the other big players, but I am certain that real monetization will come later: for now, the bulk of the story remains the iPhone and services, certainly not AI.

Warning: high multiples

Let's move on to the sore point: the numbers. I'll say it right away, Apple is not cheap, and there's no doubt about that. As I said, the P/E ratio is around 35 times earnings, and the EV/EBITDA is around 25x. We are at or near historic highs, so it is reasonable to ask, “If I buy today, am I buying at a high price?” The answer I can give you is that Apple deserves at least part of this premium valuation, and the improved outlook reduces the risk of a drastic compression of multiples.

At one time (pre-2016), it was priced as a cyclical hardware company, with a P/E ratio of around 12-15x.

Today, we cannot consider Apple solely as a hardware company, as we did in the past, but rather as a consumer company that you cannot do without. Its user base is huge and loyal, guaranteeing recurring cash flows. This is probably why the market values it at higher multiples than in the past. The low interest rates we have experienced in recent years of monetary easing have also contributed to this. In fact, if we look at the period between 2019 and 2021, the average P/E ratio first rose above 20x during this period, reaching and almost exceeding 45x. Today, we are far from those levels, but we remain at the high end of the historical range, just below the all-time highs.

However, the quality is there, and as I said, it should be rewarded. It is one of the top companies in the S&P 500 index with the highest ROIC, at around 50%, with a competitive advantage that is difficult to undermine (integrated hardware-software-services ecosystem, enormous brand strength, and consumer loyalty at the highest levels), and above all, stable earnings and cash flow, which is not always easy to find in tech stocks.

This justifies a premium.

The company can therefore afford to return tens of billions a year to shareholders and invest as much as it needs in R&D (and let's hope it's enough to stay in the race!). The buybacks (which I viewed negatively in my July analysis because they seemed disproportionate to AI investments) are the most massive around. The float is reduced by an additional 2-3% each year, which alone adds a couple of percentage points of EPS growth without the need to increase total earnings. I'm not saying that Apple is undervalued, because it clearly isn't. I'm just saying that in a well-diversified, long-term portfolio, it makes sense to pay a premium for this type of quality. Apple's valuation remains a risk, but in the risk/return balance, I am now more inclined to accept it given the renewed momentum of the business.

Services and AI

Apple has a strength that not everyone has fully understood, namely a huge customer base—we're talking about over a billion people. It already exploits this “audience” in part through payment services (Apple Pay) and content (Apple TV+), etc. It has also recently entered the advertising sector, where Apple is quietly building an advertising business (ads on the App Store, Apple News, and potentially Apple Maps). If it decides to push into this field—always with caution so as not to damage its privacy reputation—it could tap into a portion of the huge digital advertising budgets. The company has an audience of over a billion premium users; there are many ways to better monetize this audience, and Apple has only explored a few of them so far. Each new successful service adds subscribers and ARR (annual recurring revenue). I believe it is possible that within 2-3 years, Services will exceed $120-$130 billion per year, approaching 30% of revenues. This would also bring Apple's operating margin to new highs (today it is around 30%, it could rise to 33-35% thanks to the richer mix). An interesting, hidden advantage.

The sore point, which, however, becomes a sort of call option on the stock, is Artificial Intelligence. Please don't hold it against me, but I still have some doubts, and I'm not convinced that AI will deliver results in the short term. I'm skeptical. Nevertheless, I see untapped potential. As a consumer and Apple user, I would be willing to pay for an additional premium service if the company offered AI services. We know that Apple customers are willing to pay for quality and privacy, and offering a reliable and private AI assistant could create a new revenue stream. I must say that I am eagerly awaiting this moment, as it is one of the areas where AI would make the most sense. For now, Siri is not up to speed, and it is time for them to wake up to this fact.

I'm back

From a technical analysis perspective, I am therefore starting to accumulate at these prices, aware that I am not getting the deal of the century given the levels. But I will start with small amounts. I would be more confident about entering on a slight downturn, in the $243 area (-5% from current levels). In that area, I would step on the accelerator with more confidence.

data.title: Core PCE Inflation Holds Steady At 2.9% In August, As Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The Fed’s preferred inflation gauge, the core PCE price index, rose 2.9% year-over-year in August, the highest level since February. This was consistent with the forecast and a slight pickup from July. On a monthly basis, the core index was up 0.2%, as expected.

The headline index was up 2.7% year-over-year, the highest level since April 2024. This was consistent with the forecast and marked an acceleration from June's 2.6% reading. On a monthly basis, the index was up 0.3%, as expected.

PCE Price Index

Personal consumption expenditures (PCE) measures and tracks changes for all domestic personal consumption. Core PCE measures the changes in personal consumption less food and energy, making it less volatile than the headline PCE. The PCE Price Index is calculated using PCE data and is a key way to measure changes in purchasing trends and inflation.

The adjacent thumbnail gives us a close-up of the trend in YoY core PCE since January 2012. The first string of red data points highlights the 12 consecutive months when core PCE hovered in a narrow range around its interim low. The second string highlights the lower range from late 2014 through 2015. Core PCE shifted higher in 2016 with a decline in 2017, 2019, and 2020, with a major jump in 2022.

The following chart shows the monthly year-over-year change in the Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) price index since 2000. An overlay of the core PCE price index is also included, with the highlighted line on the chart representing the 2% benchmark for core inflation.

For a long-term perspective, here are the same two metrics spanning five decades.

Inflation: Fed's 2% Target

The Fed traditionally uses the PCE Price Index as their preferred inflation gauge; however, another common measure of inflation is the Consumer Price Index.

In their latest meeting, the Fed implemented a widely anticipated 25 basis point cut to the federal funds rate (FFR), bringing it to a range of 4.00%-4.25%. This marked the first rate cut of the year, with two more quarter-point cuts anticipated before year-end.

The statement from the meeting revealed the committee believes "inflation has moved up and remains somewhat elevated," but that they are strongly committed to returning inflation to its 2% objective.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
